title: Shipping AI
subtitle: Inference, Serving, Quantization, and the Economics of Production
theme: The day AI meets the spreadsheet — how to take a model from a demo to a profitable product.
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between training and inference, and why inference is where the money is spent
Describe quantization (INT8/INT4) and distillation, and the quality/cost trade-offs they create
Understand the serving stack: KV-cache, continuous batching, and what drives latency vs throughput
Reason about GPU economics — why VRAM and utilization, not just FLOPs, decide your bill
Decide between on-device and cloud inference for a given product
Budget an AI product end to end and apply LLMOps/observability to keep it healthy in production
storyHook: Your model is brilliant. It tops the evals, it's grounded, it's safe. You launch. Then the first invoice arrives — and it's larger than your payroll. This is the chapter nobody puts in the keynote: the unglamorous, ruthless, deeply satisfying engineering of making AI cheap enough to give away and fast enough that people don't leave. Today you learn the tricks that separate a viral demo that bankrupts its founder from a product that prints money.

slides.title: The Bill That Ends the Honeymoon
slides.body:
## Training gets the headlines. Inference pays the bills.

Everyone talks about the millions spent *training* a frontier model. But for a company *using* AI, almost all the cost — and all the user experience — lives in **inference**: running the model to answer each request, millions of times a day.

```
TRAINING:   one-time (huge), done by the lab     -> CapEx, occasional
INFERENCE:  every single user request, forever    -> OpEx, relentless
```

## Today's mission: make inference fast and cheap

We attack from four angles:
1. **Shrink the model** — quantization, distillation. (Fewer/smaller numbers.)
2. **Serve it cleverly** — KV-cache, batching. (Waste no GPU cycle.)
3. **Place it wisely** — cloud vs on-device. (Right hardware, right place.)
4. **Run it like a business** — LLMOps, observability, budgeting. (Know your unit economics.)

> **The mantra of production AI:** A model that's 5% smarter but 10x more expensive usually loses to the cheaper one. In production, **cost-per-quality** beats raw quality.
slides.speakerNotes: Open with the founder horror story — the viral demo that bankrupts its creator. Set the frame: today is about OpEx, not CapEx. Ask: 'If your AI feature costs $2 per user per day and you charge $10/month, what happens?' Let the math sink in.
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, inference cost is the central economic question of the AI industry — the GPU shortage and the race-to-the-bottom on per-token pricing have made efficient serving a survival skill, not an optimization.

slides.title: Quantization: Same Brain, Fewer Bits
slides.body:
## The core idea

A model is billions of numbers (weights). By default each is stored in high precision — 16 or 32 bits. **Quantization** stores them in *fewer* bits — 8 (INT8), 4 (INT4), even lower — accepting a tiny loss of precision in exchange for a big win in size and speed.

## The analogy
Think of describing a color. You *could* use a precise scientific wavelength (32-bit). Or you could say 'sky blue' (4-bit). For most purposes, 'sky blue' is good enough — and far cheaper to store and transmit. Quantization is rounding the model's numbers to 'good enough.'

```
FP16  weight: 0.0273438   (16 bits)
INT8  weight: 0.027       (8 bits)   ~2x smaller
INT4  weight: 0.03        (4 bits)   ~4x smaller
```

## Why it's a big deal

| Benefit | Why |
|---------|-----|
| **Smaller memory** | 4-bit = ~1/4 the VRAM. A model that needed 4 GPUs may fit on 1. |
| **Faster** | Less data to move; memory bandwidth is often the bottleneck. |
| **Cheaper** | Fewer/cheaper GPUs, lower power. |
| **Enables edge** | 4-bit models can run on a laptop or phone. |

## The catch
- **Quality degrades** as you go lower — usually gracefully at INT8, more noticeably at INT4 and below.
- Not all parts of the model tolerate it equally (some layers/outliers are sensitive).
- **Two flavors:** *Post-Training Quantization (PTQ)* — quantize an existing model cheaply; *Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)* — train with quantization in mind for better quality.

> **Rule of thumb 2026:** INT8 is often near-free quality-wise; INT4 is the sweet spot for running big models on modest hardware; below that, test carefully.
slides.speakerNotes: The 'sky blue vs wavelength' analogy makes quantization instantly intuitive. Emphasize VRAM savings (4x at INT4) as the headline — it's what lets big models run on small hardware. Ask: 'Would you trade 1% accuracy for running on 1 GPU instead of 4?'
slides.marketConnection: Quantized open-weight models (GGUF/INT4 and similar formats) made local AI mainstream by 2026 — running capable models on consumer laptops and phones is now routine, fueling the on-device wave and tools like local model runners.

slides.title: Distillation: The Apprentice Learns from the Master
slides.body:
## Teaching a small model to imitate a big one

**Knowledge distillation** trains a small 'student' model to mimic a large, capable 'teacher' model. The student learns not just the right answers, but the teacher's *nuanced probability distribution* over answers — its 'soft' judgments — and ends up far better than if it had been trained from scratch.

```
BIG TEACHER  (slow, expensive, smart)
      |  generates answers / soft labels
      v
SMALL STUDENT  (fast, cheap, surprisingly capable)
```

## Why distillation matters commercially
- A distilled model can be **10x smaller and cheaper** while keeping much of the teacher's quality on the target tasks.
- It's how labs ship 'mini'/'flash'/'haiku'-style tiers: small, fast, cheap models that punch above their weight.
- It pairs beautifully with quantization — distill *then* quantize for maximum shrinkage.

## The strategic pattern: the model cascade / router
In production you rarely use one model. You use a **portfolio**:
```
Easy/cheap request   -> small distilled model   (covers the 80%)
Hard/ambiguous request -> big flagship model     (the expensive 20%)
```
A router sends each request to the cheapest model that can handle it. This single pattern can cut a production bill by more than half.

> **The insight:** You don't need a genius for every task. Most requests are easy. Distillation gives you a cheap workhorse for the easy 80%, and you reserve the expensive flagship for the hard 20%.
slides.speakerNotes: Distillation = apprentice imitating master. The commercially crucial idea is the CASCADE/ROUTER — don't use a sledgehammer for every nail. Ask: 'In your use case, what fraction of requests are genuinely hard?' Most realize it's small.
slides.marketConnection: The 2024-2026 tiered model lineups (flagship + mini/flash/haiku variants) are distillation in action; many of the cheapest, fastest 2026 models are distilled, and 'model routing' is now a standard cost-optimization product category.

slides.title: Inside Serving: The KV-Cache
slides.body:
## Why generation seems to 'remember' what it just wrote

LLMs generate one token at a time. Naively, to produce token #100, the model would re-process tokens #1-99 from scratch. That's catastrophic waste. The fix is the **KV-cache.**

## What it is
During attention, each token produces **Key** and **Value** vectors. The KV-cache **stores** them so that when generating the next token, the model reuses the past Ks and Vs instead of recomputing them.

```
Without KV-cache: token 100 re-reads & recomputes tokens 1..99   (quadratic waste)
With KV-cache:    token 100 reuses cached K,V for 1..99, computes only itself
```

This is *the* reason text generation is fast enough to feel like typing.

## The catch: the cache eats memory
The KV-cache grows with **sequence length x batch size x model size.** For long contexts and many concurrent users, the KV-cache can consume *more VRAM than the model weights themselves.*

This is why:
- **Long context is expensive at serve time**, not just at training (remember Day 16).
- Serving systems fight hard to manage cache memory — innovations like **PagedAttention** (the idea behind vLLM) treat KV-cache like virtual memory pages to avoid waste and fit more users per GPU.
- Techniques like **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)** and **Multi-Query Attention** shrink the KV-cache by sharing keys/values across attention heads.

> **Mental model:** The model weights are the chef; the KV-cache is the chef's short-term memory of the current order. The longer and more numerous the orders, the more memory you need — fast.
slides.speakerNotes: KV-cache is the single most important serving concept. Land two points: (1) it's why generation is fast, (2) it's a hidden, growing memory cost that long context and many users blow up. Mention vLLM/PagedAttention as the famous real-world fix. Ask: 'Why does a 1M-token chat cost more to SERVE, not just to read?'
slides.marketConnection: Serving engines like vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and similar became core infrastructure by 2026 — KV-cache management (PagedAttention, GQA) is a key reason per-token inference prices kept falling even as context windows grew.

slides.title: Batching, Latency, and Throughput
slides.body:
## The two numbers that fight each other

Every serving decision is a tug-of-war between:

- **Latency** — how long *one* user waits. (User experience.)
- **Throughput** — how many requests you serve *per second* across everyone. (Cost efficiency.)

GPUs are massively parallel — they're happiest doing many things at once. So serving one request at a time wastes most of the chip.

## Batching: fill the GPU
**Batching** means processing many requests together in one GPU pass. More requests per pass = higher throughput = lower cost per request. But waiting to fill a batch adds latency for early arrivals.

### The breakthrough: continuous (in-flight) batching
Classic 'static' batching waits for a whole batch to finish before starting the next — slow requests stall fast ones. **Continuous batching** lets requests join and leave the batch *dynamically*, as soon as slots free up.
```
Static batch:     [====slow====][fast.] ...all wait for the slowest
Continuous batch: fast requests leave early, new ones slot in instantly
```
This dramatically raises GPU utilization without trashing latency — a foundational trick of modern serving.

## The metrics you'll actually report
- **TTFT** — Time To First Token (how fast it *starts* answering; perceived responsiveness).
- **TPOT / inter-token latency** — time per subsequent token (how fast it *streams*).
- **Throughput** — total tokens/sec across all users (drives your cost).

> **The trade-off in one line:** You can have low latency OR high throughput cheaply — pushing both to the limit costs money. Production is choosing the right point on that curve for *your* product.
slides.speakerNotes: Make the latency-vs-throughput tension visceral: it's UX vs cost. Continuous batching is the hero. Introduce TTFT as the metric users actually feel (a chatbot that starts fast feels fast even if total time is similar). Ask: 'For a live chat vs a nightly report job, which matters more — latency or throughput?'
slides.marketConnection: Continuous batching (popularized by vLLM and adopted across serving stacks) is a primary reason 2026 inference is so cheap; TTFT and tokens/sec are now standard SLAs in enterprise AI contracts.

slides.title: GPU Economics: It's the VRAM, Stupid
slides.body:
## The most counterintuitive lesson in AI infrastructure

Beginners obsess over raw compute (FLOPs). Practitioners obsess over two other things: **VRAM** and **utilization.**

### 1. VRAM is usually the gatekeeper
You can't run a model that doesn't *fit in memory.* A model's footprint = **weights + KV-cache + overhead.** If it doesn't fit on your GPU's VRAM, you need more (or bigger) GPUs — which is the single biggest cost multiplier.
```
Rough weight memory:  params x bytes-per-param
  70B model @ FP16 (2 bytes)  ~140 GB  -> multiple GPUs
  70B model @ INT4 (0.5 byte) ~35 GB   -> maybe one big GPU
```
This is *why* quantization is economically magical: it changes how many GPUs you need to buy.

### 2. Utilization is where money leaks
A GPU you rent but only use 20% of the time still costs 100%. **The enemy is idle silicon.** Batching, autoscaling, and good routing exist to keep expensive GPUs *busy.*

## The buy vs. rent question
| Option | When it wins |
|--------|-------------|
| **API (per-token)** | Spiky/low volume, fast to start, no ops burden |
| **Rented cloud GPUs** | Steady high volume, need control/customization |
| **Owned hardware** | Very high sustained volume, data residency, long horizon |

## The 2026 reality: scarcity
High-end accelerators (the H100/H200/B200-class and their rivals) have been **supply-constrained and expensive.** Access to GPUs is a strategic asset; many companies' AI roadmaps are gated not by ideas but by **chip allocation.**

> **The bottom line:** Your AI bill is decided less by 'how smart' and more by 'does it fit in VRAM' and 'are the GPUs ever idle.'
slides.speakerNotes: The keeper line: 'It's the VRAM, stupid.' Two ideas: fitting in memory (quantization changes GPU count) and utilization (idle GPUs burn money). Tie back to Day 16 (active vs total params) and quantization. Ask: 'You rent a GPU 24/7 but traffic is 9-5. Where's your money going?'
slides.marketConnection: The 2025-2026 GPU shortage made chip access a board-level strategic issue; cloud GPU pricing, reserved-capacity deals, and the rise of alternative accelerators (and inference-specialized chips) are direct consequences shaping who can afford to compete.

slides.title: On-Device vs. Cloud: Where Should the Brain Live?
slides.body:
## A real architectural fork in 2026

For years, all AI ran in the cloud. Now, thanks to quantization, distillation, and better chips (NPUs in phones and laptops), a serious option exists: **run the model on the user's device.**

## The trade-off table

| Dimension | On-Device (edge) | Cloud |
|-----------|------------------|-------|
| **Privacy** | Excellent — data never leaves device | Data sent to servers |
| **Latency** | Very low — no network round-trip | Network adds delay |
| **Cost to you** | ~Free — user's hardware pays | You pay per request, forever |
| **Offline** | Works with no internet | Needs connectivity |
| **Capability** | Limited by device (small models) | Full frontier-scale models |
| **Updates** | Hard — must push to devices | Instant — update server-side |

## The emerging pattern: hybrid
The pragmatic 2026 answer is usually **both**:
```
Small on-device model  -> handles common, private, low-latency tasks
         (escalates hard requests to...)
Large cloud model      -> handles the heavy, rare, complex requests
```
Phone makers and OS vendors ship on-device models for everyday tasks (summarize this text, transcribe, autocomplete) and route the hard stuff to the cloud — often with privacy guarantees on the cloud side too.

> **Design principle:** Push work to the edge when *privacy, latency, cost, or offline* matter; keep it in the cloud when you need *raw capability or instant updates.* Most mature products do both.
slides.speakerNotes: This is a very practical, decision-oriented slide. The cost row is the eye-opener: on-device, the USER's hardware pays — that's enormous for margins. Stress the hybrid pattern as the real-world answer. Ask: 'A keyboard autocomplete vs a legal research tool — where does each brain live?'
slides.marketConnection: On-device AI went mainstream in 2025-2026 as phone/PC makers shipped NPUs and built-in small models; the hybrid edge-plus-cloud architecture (e.g., on-device assistants escalating to cloud) is now the dominant consumer pattern and a major privacy selling point.

slides.title: LLMOps: Running AI Like a Grown-Up
slides.body:
## DevOps grew up; meet its AI cousin

Shipping a model is the *start*, not the end. **LLMOps** is the discipline of operating AI in production reliably — the unglamorous work that keeps a product alive.

## The pillars of LLMOps

### 1. Observability — you can't fix what you can't see
Log every request: prompt, response, latency, **token counts (= cost)**, model version, and errors. Without this you're flying blind and your bill is a mystery.

### 2. Evaluation in production
Your Day 17 eval set isn't one-time. Run **continuous/online evals** on live traffic, sample responses for quality, and watch for **drift** (the model or the world changed and quality slipped).

### 3. Cost & token monitoring
Track **cost per request, per user, per feature.** Set alerts. A single buggy retry loop or a runaway agent can 10x your bill overnight — observability catches it.

### 4. Prompt & model versioning
Prompts are code. Version them. A/B test changes. When you swap model versions, **regression-test** against your eval set — a 'better' new model can be worse for *your* task.

### 5. Caching & guardrails (the cost-and-safety layer)
- **Semantic caching** — if two users ask the same thing, serve the cached answer. Free latency and cost savings.
- **Guardrails** (from Day 17) live here in the request path too.

### 6. Feedback loops
Capture thumbs up/down and real outcomes; feed them back into eval sets and fine-tuning. The product gets smarter as it's used.

> **The mindset shift:** A model in production is a *living system* with a cost meter running every second. LLMOps is how you keep it accurate, fast, safe, and affordable — at the same time.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame LLMOps as DevOps for AI. The two pillars to stress: observability (especially token/cost logging) and continuous eval (catching drift). The 'runaway agent 10x's your bill' line scares people into caring. Ask: 'What's the FIRST metric you'd put on your AI dashboard?' (Cost per request is a great answer.)
slides.marketConnection: LLMOps became a defined category in 2025-2026 with a full tooling ecosystem (observability platforms, prompt management, online eval, semantic caching, gateways) — enterprises now expect this layer as standard, not optional.

slides.title: Budgeting an AI Product, Line by Line
slides.body:
## Let's actually do the math

The skill that makes you indispensable: estimating an AI feature's cost *before* you build it. Here's the napkin model.

## The core formula
```
Cost per request = (input_tokens  x input_price)
                 + (output_tokens x output_price)

Monthly cost     = cost_per_request x requests_per_user x active_users
```
(Output tokens usually cost more than input tokens — generation is the expensive part.)

## A worked example: a support chatbot
```
Assume (illustrative API pricing):
  input:  $0.50 per 1M tokens
  output: $1.50 per 1M tokens

Per conversation:
  input  (history + RAG docs):  4,000 tokens -> 4,000  x $0.50/1M = $0.0020
  output (the answer):            500 tokens -> 500   x $1.50/1M = $0.00075
  cost per conversation ~ $0.00275

Scale:
  10,000 users x 5 conversations/day x 30 days = 1,500,000 conversations/mo
  monthly cost ~ 1,500,000 x $0.00275 = ~$4,125 / month
```

## The levers that change everything
| Lever | Effect |
|-------|--------|
| **Smaller/distilled model** | Cheaper per token (often 5-20x) |
| **Quantization (self-hosted)** | Fewer GPUs -> lower fixed cost |
| **Model routing (cascade)** | Big model only for hard 20% |
| **Semantic caching** | Repeated questions become ~free |
| **Trim the context** | Fewer input tokens (RAG > stuffing) |
| **Cap output length** | Output is the pricey part |

## The unit-economics question that decides survival
```
Revenue per user per month   vs   AI cost per user per month
```
If a user pays you $10/month and costs $12 in AI, **every new customer makes you poorer.** This single comparison has killed real startups.

> **The professional habit:** before you build, estimate cost-per-request and cost-per-active-user. Then ask: does the business model survive it?
slides.speakerNotes: This is the most practically valuable slide of the entire day — walk through the arithmetic slowly on the board. Let them feel that small per-request numbers x scale = real money. End on the unit-economics line: AI cost per user must be below revenue per user. Ask the class to estimate one of THEIR ideas live.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, falling per-token prices (driven by MoE, quantization, and serving advances) made many AI features finally profitable — but unit economics still kill products, and 'cost per active user' is a metric every AI startup now lives or dies by.

slides.title: The Full Picture: From Weights to Wallet
slides.body:
## How the whole shipping stack fits together

Everything today connects into one pipeline that turns a trained model into an affordable product:

```
  TRAINED MODEL (big, expensive)
        |
  [ DISTILL ]   -> smaller student model for the easy 80%
        |
  [ QUANTIZE ]  -> INT8/INT4: fits in less VRAM, runs cheaper
        |
  [ SERVE ]     -> KV-cache + continuous batching + PagedAttention
        |         keep GPUs BUSY (utilization), watch VRAM
        |
  [ PLACE ]     -> on-device for the cheap/private/fast path
        |         cloud for the heavy/rare path  (hybrid)
        |
  [ ROUTE ]     -> cheap model for easy reqs, flagship for hard ones
        |
  [ OPERATE ]   -> LLMOps: observe, eval, cache, version, alert on cost
        |
   PROFITABLE PRODUCT
```

## The three questions that decode any AI deployment
1. **Does it fit?** (VRAM — drives how many GPUs / whether it runs on-device.)
2. **Is the hardware busy?** (Utilization + batching — drives cost efficiency.)
3. **Do the unit economics work?** (Cost per active user < revenue per active user.)

Master those three and you can sit in any architecture or budget review and ask the questions that matter.

> Day 16 made models efficient. Day 17 made them trustworthy. Day 18 made them *shippable.* Together, that's the full journey from a 2017 research paper to a 2026 product line.
slides.speakerNotes: Synthesis slide — walk the pipeline top to bottom and show how every earlier concept slots in. Have the class recite the three decoding questions (fit / busy / economics). This is the durable mental model they leave with.
slides.marketConnection: This pipeline is exactly how 2026 AI companies operate — distill, quantize, serve efficiently, place wisely, route, and operate with LLMOps — and it's why AI features that were unaffordable in 2023 are everyday products today.

slides.title: Quick Check
slides.body:
## Test your shipping instincts

**Q1.** Why is inference, not training, the dominant cost for most companies *using* AI?

**Q2.** INT4 quantization roughly quarters memory. Name the headline benefit and the headline risk.

**Q3.** What is the KV-cache, why does it make generation fast, and why does it make long context expensive *at serve time*?

**Q4.** Explain continuous batching and the latency-vs-throughput trade-off in one breath.

**Q5.** A user pays $8/month; your AI cost per user is $9/month. What's the term for the problem, and name two levers to fix it.

---

### Answer key
1. **Inference runs on every request, forever (OpEx)**; training is a one-time cost mostly borne by the lab. Usage scales the bill.
2. **Benefit:** ~4x less VRAM -> fewer GPUs, runs on edge, cheaper/faster. **Risk:** quality degradation (more pronounced at INT4 and below; test it).
3. The **KV-cache** stores past Key/Value vectors so each new token reuses them instead of recomputing — that's why generation is fast. It **grows with sequence length x users**, so long contexts and many concurrent users consume huge VRAM at serve time.
4. **Continuous batching** lets requests join/leave a GPU batch dynamically to maximize utilization; **batching raises throughput (lower cost) but can add latency** — production picks the right point on that curve.
5. **Broken unit economics** (negative gross margin per user). Levers: distill/use a smaller model, quantize/self-host, model routing/cascade, semantic caching, trim context, cap output length — any two.
slides.speakerNotes: Rapid-fire. Q5 (unit economics) is the must-get — it ties the whole day together commercially. Q3 (KV-cache) is the technical credibility check.
slides.marketConnection: These five questions are the literacy test for anyone making AI build-vs-buy or architecture decisions in 2026 — they map directly to real cost reviews.

slides.title: Recap & Bridge
slides.body:
## What we shipped today

- **Inference is the relentless cost** — every request, forever. Optimizing it is the whole game.
- **Shrink the model:** **quantization** (INT8/INT4 — ~2-4x smaller, fits less VRAM) and **distillation** (a small student imitates a big teacher).
- **Serve it cleverly:** the **KV-cache** makes generation fast but eats memory; **continuous batching** maximizes GPU utilization; you trade **latency vs throughput.**
- **GPU economics:** it's about **VRAM (does it fit?)** and **utilization (is it busy?)**, not just FLOPs — amid a real 2026 chip shortage.
- **Place it wisely:** **on-device vs cloud**, with **hybrid** as the mature answer.
- **Run it like a business:** **LLMOps** (observability, online eval, cost monitoring, versioning, caching) and **budgeting** down to **cost per active user vs revenue per active user.**

## The one sentence to remember
> A demo proves a model *can* work; production proves it can work *fast, cheap, and reliably enough to sell* — and that engineering, not the model, is where most AI products are won or lost.

You now hold the full arc: efficient (Day 16), trustworthy (Day 17), and shippable (Day 18). What remains is putting it all together into real systems and the frontier ahead — that's where we go next.
slides.speakerNotes: Close with energy and pride — the class has just gone from architecture to economics, the complete builder's toolkit. Land the keeper sentence. Then tee up what's next: agents, multimodality, and the June 2026 frontier where all of this converges.
slides.marketConnection: Efficiency, trust, and economics are the three disciplines every shipped AI product in June 2026 must master — and the teams that understand all three are the ones building durable businesses rather than viral demos.

keyTakeaways: Inference (every request, forever — OpEx) dominates cost for AI users, not training; cost-per-quality beats raw quality in production

Quantization (INT8/INT4) shrinks models ~2-4x, cutting VRAM and cost and enabling on-device — at some quality risk that grows below INT4; distillation trains a cheap small student to mimic a big teacher
The KV-cache makes generation fast by reusing past Key/Value vectors, but it grows with context length and concurrency — making long context and many users expensive at SERVE time
Continuous batching maximizes GPU utilization; serving is a constant latency (UX) vs throughput (cost) trade-off, measured via TTFT and tokens/sec
GPU economics is about VRAM (does it fit?) and utilization (is it busy?), not just FLOPs — quantization changes how many GPUs you need; idle GPUs burn money; chips were scarce in 2025-26

Ship like a business: choose on-device vs cloud (often hybrid), route easy requests to cheap models, run LLMOps (observability, online eval, cost monitoring, caching), and ensure cost per active user < revenue per active user
marketSnapshot2026: By June 2026, inference economics is arguably the most important commercial force in AI. The combination of MoE, quantization, distillation, and serving advances (vLLM/PagedAttention, continuous batching, TensorRT-LLM) drove per-token prices down dramatically year over year, finally turning many AI features profitable. Quantized open-weight models (INT4/GGUF) made local AI mainstream, and phone/PC makers shipped on-device models on NPUs, normalizing the hybrid edge-plus-cloud pattern as both a cost and privacy play. Tiered model lineups (flagship + mini/flash/haiku) — distillation in production — let teams route cheap models for the easy majority and reserve flagships for hard cases. Yet the high-end GPU shortage (H100/H200/B200-class and rivals) kept chip access a strategic, board-level constraint, fueling reserved-capacity deals and a wave of inference-specialized accelerators. LLMOps matured into a full tooling category — observability, online eval, prompt/version management, semantic caching, and AI gateways are now expected infrastructure. Above all, 'cost per active user vs revenue per active user' became the metric AI startups live or die by; falling token prices help, but broken unit economics still quietly kill products.
homework: Take an AI product idea (yours or a hypothetical, e.g., a study-notes summarizer or a support bot). Build a one-page cost model: estimate input and output tokens per request, multiply by realistic per-token prices to get cost-per-request, then scale to monthly cost at an assumed user count and usage. Then propose at least three concrete cost-reduction levers from today (e.g., distill to a smaller model, quantize and self-host, add model routing, semantic caching, trim context, cap output) and re-estimate the savings. Finally, state your assumed revenue per user and judge: do the unit economics survive? Bring the spreadsheet.
nextDayTeaser: You can now build models that are efficient, trustworthy, AND shippable — the complete engineer's toolkit. Next, we let them off the leash: agents that plan and act, tools and multimodality, and the wide-open June 2026 frontier where everything we've learned converges into systems that don't just answer — they DO. The arc that began with one 2017 paper is about to reach the edge of what's possible today.
